---
title: "2.2 Cointegration"
subtitle: "Chapter 02. The Basics of Mean Reversion"

format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    number-sections: true
    code-fold: show
    code-tools: false
include-after-body:
  - ../../../../styles/web-note-modal.html
---

::: {.section-output-links}
[Slides](video-lesson-slides.qmd){.action-video aria-label="Open video lesson slides" data-modal="video" data-label="Video lesson slides" data-tooltip="Slides"}
<span aria-hidden="true">/</span>
[PDF](pdf-notes.qmd){.action-pdf aria-label="Open PDF notes" data-tooltip="PDF"}
<span aria-hidden="true">/</span>
[Markdown](downloads/section.json){.action-markdown aria-label="Open raw markdown viewer" data-modal="markdown" data-label="Markdown" data-tooltip="Markdown" data-markdown-src="downloads/section.json"}
<span aria-hidden="true">/</span>
[Bundle](downloads/section-bundle.zip){.action-bundle aria-label="Download markdown bundle" data-tooltip="Bundle"}
:::

# Cointegration Solves the Scarcity Problem of Stationary Assets

By the end of Section 2.1, one difficulty is obvious: truly stationary traded
price series are rare. Chan's answer is that traders are not limited to
prefabricated price series. They can create a new series by taking a linear
combination of several nonstationary assets. If that combination is stationary,
the input assets are called cointegrated.

For two assets, the most familiar case is a pair trade. For a larger set of
assets, the same idea generalizes to a basket. The core mathematical object is
always the same: a weighted portfolio whose net market value behaves like a
mean-reverting series even though each component price does not.

# Hedge Ratios Define the Portfolio Being Tested

Suppose we have price series $y_1, y_2, \ldots, y_n$. A candidate portfolio
price is a weighted sum

$$
y_{\text{port}} = h_1 y_1 + h_2 y_2 + \cdots + h_n y_n.
$$

The coefficients $h_i$ are the hedge ratios. In equity language they are share
counts; in futures or ETF language they are contract or unit weights. A negative
weight means a short position.

The central difficulty is that stationarity is a property of the weighted
combination, not of the raw assets individually. Most weight choices will not
produce a stationary portfolio. Cointegration testing is therefore partly a
search for the right hedge ratios.

# CADF Uses an Estimated Hedge Ratio and Then Tests the Residual

The Engle-Granger logic, which Chan implements through the Cointegrated ADF
(CADF) test, works in two steps for a pair:

1. estimate the hedge ratio by regressing one series on the other
2. test whether the residual spread is stationary

For a pair $(x_t, y_t)$, a regression of $y_t$ on $x_t$ gives

$$
y_t = h x_t + c + u_t,
$$

where the residual

$$
u_t = y_t - h x_t - c
$$

is the candidate stationary portfolio up to the constant offset. The CADF test
then applies the ADF logic to $u_t$ rather than to one raw price series.

This solves the main problem left open by ordinary ADF testing. We do not know
the correct hedge ratio in advance, so we cannot test the pair portfolio until
we estimate the combination first.

# Why CADF Is Different from Testing a Naive Spread

Without the regression step, a trader might test an arbitrary spread such as
$y_t - x_t$ or a dollar-neutral construction chosen for convenience. Chan's
point is that cointegration does not guarantee that every linear combination is
stationary. Only specific combinations work.

The regression estimate is therefore not cosmetic. It is the mechanism that
turns two drifting assets into a candidate stationary series. Once that series
is built, the ADF logic from the previous section applies again.

# The EWA-EWC Example Shows the Full CADF Workflow

Chan uses EWA and EWC, ETFs tied to Australian and Canadian equities, as a
practical illustration. Because both economies are strongly commodity-linked,
the pair is a plausible cointegration candidate.

The workflow is:

1. regress EWC on EWA to estimate a hedge ratio
2. form the residual series $y_t - h x_t$
3. run CADF on that residual series

The reported CADF statistic is about $-3.64$, while the 5 percent critical
value is about $-3.359$. Because $-3.64$ is more negative, the null that the
residual has a unit root is rejected at the 95 percent level. In Chan's
language, EWA and EWC are cointegrating with 95 percent certainty.

# CADF Is Order Dependent

This is one of the most important caveats in the section. If we regress EWC on
EWA, we get one hedge ratio. If we regress EWA on EWC, we get another. Those
two estimates are generally not exact reciprocals, and only one direction may
produce the more stationary residual.

That means CADF is order dependent. The test is not asking only whether the two
assets move together. It is also inheriting the asymmetry of the regression used
to estimate the portfolio weights.

Chan's practical recommendation is blunt: if CADF is the tool being used, try
each variable as the independent one and keep the ordering that yields the most
negative test statistic.

# Johansen Generalizes the Framework to a Multivariate System

CADF works naturally for a pair, but traders often want to test a basket of
three or more assets. Chan then introduces the Johansen test by lifting the
univariate ADF-style dynamics into vector form:

$$
\Delta Y(t) = \Lambda Y(t-1) + M
  + A_1 \Delta Y(t-1) + \cdots + A_k \Delta Y(t-k) + \varepsilon_t.
\tag{2.7}
$$

Here:

- $Y(t)$ is now a vector of price series
- $\Lambda$ and $A_i$ are coefficient matrices
- $M$ is a constant offset vector

The same intuition remains in force. If the next move of the vector does not
depend on the current level, then there is no mean-reverting long-run relation.
In the Johansen framework, that dependence is summarized by the rank of
$\Lambda$.

# The Rank of the Long-Run Matrix Counts Cointegrating Relations

Let $n$ be the number of price series and let $r = \operatorname{rank}(\Lambda)$.
Then $r$ is the number of independent cointegrating relations in the system.

This interpretation is the key reason Johansen is so useful:

- $r = 0$: no cointegration
- $r = 1$: one independent stationary portfolio
- $r > 1$: several distinct stationary portfolios exist

The test provides trace and eigen statistics, each evaluated against critical
values for null hypotheses such as $r = 0$, $r \le 1$, and so on. A trader does
not need to derive these statistics by hand; the important output is how many
cointegrating relations survive the critical-value comparisons.

# Johansen Also Produces the Hedge Ratios

The by-product that matters most in implementation is the eigenvector output.
Johansen returns eigenvectors associated with the cointegrating relations, and
those eigenvectors can be used directly as hedge ratios.

This is more powerful than CADF in two ways:

- it is not tied to one arbitrary regression ordering
- it can return multiple stationary portfolios from one basket

In practice, Chan treats the eigenvector associated with the largest eigenvalue
as the strongest relation, in the sense that it often corresponds to the
shortest half-life.

# The EWA-EWC Pair Under Johansen

Applying Johansen to the EWA-EWC pair confirms the CADF conclusion that the
pair is cointegrating. But Chan emphasizes a subtler point: with two series,
Johansen can recover two independent relations, which aligns with the idea that
reversing the dependent and independent roles in a CADF-style regression can
lead to different stationary constructions.

This is the mathematical reason Johansen is cleaner. Instead of re-running the
regression manually under different orderings, the system test extracts the full
set of relations in one step.

# The Three-Asset Example Shows Why Johansen Matters

Chan then adds IGE, an ETF of natural-resource stocks, to create a three-series
system $(\text{EWA}, \text{EWC}, \text{IGE})$. The Johansen test reports
eigenvalues and eigenvectors for the system, and Chan chooses the first
eigenvector because it corresponds to the largest eigenvalue and therefore the
strongest relation.

The stationary portfolio price is then formed as

$$
y_{\text{port}}(t) = h_1 y_1(t) + h_2 y_2(t) + h_3 y_3(t),
$$

where $(h_1,h_2,h_3)$ is the selected eigenvector.

Once this portfolio price is constructed, the analysis returns to familiar
ground. The trader can estimate $\lambda$ by regressing $\Delta y_{\text{port}}$
on $y_{\text{port}}(t-1)$ and compute the half-life exactly as in Section 2.1.

Chan reports a half-life of roughly 23 days for the selected triplet relation,
which is much shorter than the 115-day estimate for USD.CAD. That comparison is
precisely the payoff of cointegration: a basket may create a much more tradable
mean-reverting process than any one raw component.

# A Linear Mean-Reversion Strategy Can Then Be Applied to the Portfolio

Once the unit portfolio is defined by the Johansen eigenvector, Chan reuses the
same linear trading rule as before. Let $y_{\text{port}}$ be the unit-portfolio
price and let the lookback be chosen near the estimated half-life. Then

$$
\text{numUnits}_t
= -\frac{y_{\text{port},t} - \operatorname{movingAvg}(y_{\text{port},t})}
         {\operatorname{movingStd}(y_{\text{port},t})}.
$$

The actual positions in the underlying assets are those unit-portfolio weights
scaled by $\text{numUnits}_t$. In other words, the strategy does not trade the
raw ETFs one by one based on separate signals. It trades multiples of one
constructed stationary portfolio.

# Cointegration Widens the Search Space Without Eliminating Risk

Cointegration is powerful because it transforms a scarce search problem into a
richer one. The trader no longer asks only, "Which single assets are
stationary?" The trader asks, "Which combinations of related assets generate a
stationary price process?"

But the method does not make every pair or basket safe. Hedge ratios may drift,
transaction costs may matter, and a statistically cointegrated relation can
still become economically unstable. Cointegration is therefore best read as a
construction principle and a filtering device, not as a guarantee of profits.

# Crypto Notebooks Separate Data Refresh from Statistical Inspection

The `notebooks/` folder applies the same CADF and Johansen logic to shared
crypto close-price fixtures:

- `cadf-crypto-pairs.ipynb` estimates pair hedge ratios and compares both CADF
  orderings
- `johansen-crypto-basket.ipynb` tests a multivariate crypto basket and uses an
  eigenvector as portfolio weights

The notebooks read `fixtures/crypto/crypto_daily_close.csv` instead of
downloading prices during the lesson. Refresh the shared fixture data from the
repository root with:

```bash
python3 scripts/python/download-crypto-fixtures.py --source binance-monthly-archive
```

This separation keeps the notebooks reproducible while still letting the data
be updated when the network is available.

# Review Checklist

- What exact linear combination is being tested?
- Is the hedge ratio estimated, or was it chosen by convenience?
- If CADF is used, was order dependence checked explicitly?
- If Johansen is used, what rank does the test imply?
- Which eigenvector is being traded, and what half-life does its portfolio price
  imply?

# Reading Path

- Previous: [2.1 Mean Reversion and Stationarity](../01-mean-reversion-and-stationarity/web-notes.qmd)
- Next: [2.3 Pros and Cons of Mean-Reverting Strategies](../03-pros-and-cons-of-mean-reverting-strategies/web-notes.qmd)
